sentences.text: I was sewing, too.
I went to work.
And I got the two kids.
First I got my daughter.
I didn't get that room to live.
My husband got some-- a room from some woman.
She was the German [GERMAN], it's called.
And my daughter was born, I didn't have no place to go.
And he worked in a big factory.
I said, you have to do something.
I can't live like this.
[NON-ENGLISH] And she wasn't a good [NON-ENGLISH].
When my husband was by himself, she got everything.
He bring this, he bring this.
And I came, this was finished.
And when my daughter was born, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a very, very hard life.
A very hard life.
In Bytom.
Date? 27-- October 27, 1948.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1948, yeah.
My son was in '52.
What she did, one time I saw, she took the rugs by hand.
She got-- and got this bat for her.
And the dust and everything goes on the baby.
And I was crazy.
I was so hurt.
Because I got her.
It's terrible.
Eight months I was in bed.
I couldn't save the pregnancy.
Yeah, very, very difficult.
I couldn't go down, and I have to be in bed.
My husband took care of me like a child, before he went to work.
It was very hard for me.
Then he came and I said, no, I can't stay here no more.
You go to your president and tell him everything, and they give you some room for yourself.
That's what he did.
Then-- he then took him, and he told them.
It was a beautiful apartment not too far from the factory.
It was two rooms, a small kitchen on the second floor.
Over there was second, was the third floor.
And I got them a room, and I was very happy.
We bought this, this, and furnitures, a little.
Little by little I got very nice two rooms.
And not too far from the factory.
Not too far from the park.
And I got a beautiful yard in the back.
This whole time my baby was in a wagon, and fresh air.
And I look in the window.
Not like now, you can't let the kids outside.
It was very good.
And this was-- and she was a big girl.
I have to go to work.
I can't stay like this.
It was very hard.
One pension, and it was hard.
I said, I have to go to work.
[NON-ENGLISH], for I was in Bytom, the big factory.
Then--
Somebody take care of her.
I couldn't go--
Yeah.
I got for myself a room in the factory in Bytom, in the big factory.
I got the room, and I make samples.
I make men pants.
Not like they make here.
Ha.
Such beautiful pants.
And I make the pants.
Everybody was clapping.
Honest to God, how many thanks I got, from big, big factory, from the [NON-ENGLISH], I don't know, this was a big factory.
But they sent the samples over there.
And they showed this, honest to God, they couldn't believe I'd never sewed pants.
I did by myself.
But not like this.
You have to iron before you sewing.
The pants like-- the legs go like this.
My son was then was born in this month, the 17th, in 1942.
I was in Bytom the whole time, because they didn't let us out from the town, from the factory.
They need my husband over there.
Then I hear this is the last one what you can go.
Or you go, or you stay.
So I says to my husband, the kids was big.
[NON-ENGLISH] My daughter went to the high school.
My son was in the regular school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, he was in another school.
But not in the-- he wants to be-- he is now in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he is in the [NON-ENGLISH].
Special, he is a special.
He wants this little-- this little-- I said to my husband, listen, you don't want to go.
I take the two kids and I go.
They didn't send us here.
They sent us to Israel.
Oh sure.
Sure there was.
Like you hear-- all over, the semitism.
All over.
It doesn't say what.
What they can do to you, they do.
You know, because they're jealous of the Jewish people, too.
No.
I didn't got.
They was very nice to us.
They respect very much.
Because I no want to be in Poland.
I have very close friend in Poland, a woman.
And she's still in Poland.
They don't let her out.
She has a son in Sweden.
Over there she was three months, she feels very good.
Her husband passed away.
She's by herself.
She's got a daughter, and she married another Jewish guy.
That's why she left-- she stayed still in Poland.
Then the girl-- the daughter got married.
The daughter left to Germany, and she was by herself, is she now.
And that's a very sick woman.
Me?
I don't want to stay in Poland, but I know what Poland is.
What the war will show you.
I know what Poland is.
And I-- I wouldn't go to Poland now.
I wouldn't go if they gave me for free to go, I wouldn't go.
The Polack is-- I can't stand them.
I says to my husband, you don't want to go?
I'll take the two kids and I'll go.
I don't know where I'd go, but I'd go.
No.
She was in high school.
And she finished high school.
She wants to go to university.
Hm?
What year we left Poland? '69, '68. '70 we was in-- in-- '69, we was in Vienna.
Three months in Vienna.
But everybody has to go to Vienna.
So we went for three months to Vienna, and they gave us a room.
No.
They gave us papers.
They let us out from Poland.
Otherwise you can't go.
So my husband says, no, I have to go, too.
I said, very good.
Yeah.
No, you can't do this.
You have to have papers.
We came to Vienna.
We got a room.
It was a big, big house.
And she got specialy people from-- what they came from Poland.
But this was mixed marriages, and I told you something.
The mixed marriages got better like we got.
They give them everything when they came here.
Multiple organizations.
HIAS.
HIAS.
You remember, and maybe you don't, the HIAS was special to send to Israel.
We was in HIAS in Vienna.
And my brother came from Israel to us, and he said, you know what?
I don't like you go to Israel because I have a son.
He has to go in the army right away.
I was afraid.
I lost everybody in Poland.
I don't want this-- to have with him.
I don't want-- They have people they sent for an interview to America.
We was on the list.
If you got something from communists, they don't let you into United States.
Was one couple, was something, he was in the military.
I think he got something.
And he got a job here.
He got everything ready.
He couldn't go to the United States.
We got interviewed so many times.
I got separate from my husband.
They didn't find anything.
It doesn't-- they didn't-- it wasn't something like this.
It was always-- and better now, think God, you know?
Like kosher.
They tell us we go to United States.
And then we have to go to Hartford.
My God, when we came here.
And the-- then we came at the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I forgot how to call [NON-ENGLISH].
And then you come here.
Yeah.
We came here.
I didn't got to go in toilet to pay.
Oh God, I said.
And the one lady, she saw me.
She said, come on, I give the money.
She gave me the money.
I didn't got anything when I came.
The HIAS gave us some money, and they sent us to a section, not be like they sent here to United States, the Russian people invest, fill the very good sections, beautiful houses.
We went to a place, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was so crying.
My God, that I came here.
What I got in Poland, I was crying, nothing.
Then my family I got here, cousins.
One gave me the first time $300.
It was good. $300 25 years ago wasn't bad.
But he was very, very rich.
The other came, she bring us this, and this, and food, everything.
She was very good.
But she didn't got so much.
But she came almost every week.
And I went to work in Hartford.
I didn't know language, nothing.
When I have to go on bus that my daughter daughter wrote me, where I have to go.
And I came over there.
There was the Italian people.
They work.
And I talked to them, I arrive, I remember.
And my husband didn't know anything.
He doesn't want to teach the language, nothing.
He's didn't work for a long time.
Because I worked, and he stays in the home, he couldn't take this anymore.
And I was in the school, too, over there.
They let-- don't let me out from the school.
They said, Gienia, stay here.
You are so good.
And I was laughing.
They don't know nothing.
[LAUGHS] My daughter came, she sat with me, she pitched me, you know.
And for three months I was sewing.
And I got money some.
The Jewish organization gave me some money.
It wasn't so bad.
Then I don't want to stay in Hartford in this section.
I was afraid to go out.
And the school, my daughter went with me to the school.
I was afraid to go.
Everybody was afraid.
But it wasn't so bad like now.
Now is very bad.
Then my cousin says, I have a nice apartment for you.
The section this time wasn't so bad.
But this time now, it is very, very bad.
I went over there, and I take this room.
It was so bad, this room, for Jewish people.
I remember I put $500 for our rent in this apartment.
And I didn't know nothing.
They didn't tell me that I can't have for the taxes the $500.
I gave them Leonard.
He was a Jewish guy.
He took from me.
And I arrived, and I work right away.
You remember-- I don't remember shirt factory.
He had very big shirt factory.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] oh my God, I forgot.
And I went over there, this building was when I was the the
I went over there with my cousin and they said, they're going to see how I sew.
I went and I sewed.
And they saw how I sewed right away.
They take me to work.
And right away I start to work.
And I worked business day clothes.
How was the name?
I forgot.
With the names they did it, and the Germans, they always names.
I work over there with they clothes, they clothes.
I got right away on my big job.
But Jewish people.
And I work the whole time with they clothes.
And they closed-- they close anyway, this place.
I got the--
I'm not going to go.
My son has a key.
What time is it?
Oh, maybe they-- [DOORBELL]
Yeah.
Gent.
I was over there, the first one.
I showed them how to sew.
From the Holocaust?
All the time.
Now I say to myself, I don't know how I survived.
Maybe my mother [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
I was the first one.
And somebody make-- in concentration camp, they make six pair of pants, I make seven.
And I first finished like they make six.
Yeah.
I think and I says to myself, I don't know how I survived so many things.
This is-- I cannot remember everything.
You cannot remember everything.
One time one German, I got-- they beat me because somebody-- they wanted to catch somebody.
I was sure that they wanted to catch me.
I was-- I was afraid.
Why I survived?
I don't know.
I survived.
My husband, my brother.
My brother, my husband, without them, I don't know.
They wouldn't send me to Brunnlitz with my husband.
They know.
They know.
They have tapes.
I have tapes from Yale.
I have tapes here from the academy from Milford, and from Oh, yeah.
I had the girl.
She got questions from the history.
So I tell her when she wrote.
She took my picture over there.
The picture-- she kept the picture long time, then she bring this back.
I told you the truth.
I don't think I did everything.
I can't remember everything.
This is a miracle.
I don't remember.
When you was-- you showed them was nothing.
Was much, much more.
I was over there.
They wait for me.
They ask me, I says, it's all right, but not everything what it was.
They didn't show Auschwitz.
They didn't talk in there, that's how they killed the people.
